nativeRange.habitat: Small, clear, slow-moving streams, creeks, and tributaries with sandy or fine-gravel substrates; often found among leaf litter and submerged roots in shaded areas

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (critical — hard substrates damage barbels)
leaf litter (Indian almond or oak leaves)
driftwood and twisted roots for cover
low-light floating plants (amazon frogbit, water lettuce) to diffuse light

diet.notes: Micro-predator and detritivore in the wild, sifting substrate for small invertebrates, worms, and organic matter. In the aquarium, accepts high-quality micro-pellets, crushed flake, and small frozen/live foods: microworms, baby brine shrimp, Daphnia, Moina, and Grindal worms. Sinking wafers and bottom-targeted foods are essential — this species does not compete well at the water surface. Feed once to twice daily in small amounts.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (ember tetras, neon tetras, chili rasboras)
freshwater shrimp (neocaridina, caridina — compatible with adults)
avoidWith: predatory catfish (piranhas, large plecos prone to aggression)
fin-nippers that target slow-moving bottom fish
large fast-moving fish that compete aggressively for food at the bottom

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by TheJammingYam, 1024x683. Group of three on gravel showing the natural shoaling behavior of this bottom-hugging species; correct salt-and-pepper blotched flank pattern, in focus. Best representation of how the fish is kept.
Photo by D.W., 2242x1760. Tack-sharp lateral of a male foraging on fine sand substrate; clearly shows the diagnostic irregular blotched pattern and dark caudal-peduncle marking. Natural biotope look.
Photo by D.W., 3674x2291. Very high-resolution, crisp full lateral of a female on leaf litter; excellent ID reference for the mottled flank pattern and gold iridescence.

summary: The Habrosus Cory (Hoplisoma habrosum) is one of three tiny 'pygmy cory' species from Venezuelan and Colombian streams, distinguished from its relatives by true bottom-hugging behavior and an irregular blotched pattern on its flanks. Adults reach about 3.5 cm. Best kept in groups of six or more with fine sand and a gentle, well-planted setup.
careNotes:
The Habrosus Cory is the true bottom-specialist of the 'pygmy cory complex' — a group of three small Corydoras relatives routinely confused in the aquarium trade:

1. C. pygmaeus (Pygmy Cory) — a mid-water shoaler with a clear horizontal dark stripe running from snout to tail; actively swims through the water column rather than hugging the bottom.
2. C. habrosus (Salt and Pepper Cory / Habrosus Cory) — strongly bottom-oriented; identified by an irregular blotched or mottled pattern on the flanks rather than a continuous stripe; rarely leaves the substrate.
3. C. hastatus (Tail Spot Cory; see [fw-fish-130]) — a mid-water rester that perches on plant leaves; has a distinctive dark spot at the base of the tail fin with a pale halo.

True bottom-orientation is the most reliable behavioral distinguishing feature of C. habrosus from the other two species. All three are frequently mixed in retailer shipments and may be sold interchangeably under the label 'pygmy cory' — inspect specimens carefully before purchase.

Taxonomic note: A 2024 revision proposes moving a large portion of the Corydoras genus — including this species — into the resurrected genus Hoplisoma. As of 2026 this revision has not been universally adopted; both Corydoras habrosus and Hoplisoma habrosum appear in current literature and retail listings.

Husbandry: Fine sand substrate is essential — coarse gravel rapidly damages the sensitive barbels (whisker-like sensory organs used for foraging along the bottom), leading to infections and a suppression of normal foraging behavior. A group of at least six individuals (preferably ten or more) is strongly recommended; lone fish or very small groups tend to show reduced activity and increased stress. Water should be soft to moderately hard and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–12); the natural habitat can be replicated with leaf litter and driftwood to create the tannin-stained, low-mineral conditions of Orinoco and Amazonian tributaries. Keeping the temperature toward the lower end of the range (22–24°C) is associated with better long-term health. Flow should be gentle, as strong currents are stressful for this small species. Avoid copper-based medications — all callichthyid catfishes (the family to which Corydoras belongs) are known to be sensitive to copper. Regular partial water changes of around 25–30% per week help maintain water quality without large swings in chemistry.
breedingNotes: Breeding C. habrosus follows the typical Corydoras pattern but is less commonly achieved than for larger Corydoras species due to the tiny egg size. Conditioning a group with live and frozen foods (Daphnia, baby brine shrimp, microworms) for several weeks prior to a spawning attempt is recommended. Spawning is typically triggered by a water temperature drop of 2–4°C simulating the onset of the rainy season, achieved by performing a 25–30% water change with slightly cooler water. Eggs (1–2 mm diameter) are deposited singly or in small clusters on smooth surfaces including plant leaves, glass, and wood. Remove eggs or breeding adults post-spawning, as eggs and fry will be eaten. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–5 days at 24°C; fry become free-swimming within a further 1–2 days and accept infusoria and very fine foods (vinegar eels, paramecium) initially, progressing to microworms and baby brine shrimp nauplii. Fry are fragile and require pristine water conditions. Overall difficulty is moderate, driven by the very small egg and fry size and the sensitivity of early-stage juveniles to water quality.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras habrosus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Venezuela — upper Rio Meta, Rio Apure drainage; Colombia), adult size (~3.5 cm), temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 2–12 dH, habitat description (clear, slow-moving streams with sandy/leaf-litter substrate), diet (small invertebrates, organic detritus), compatibility, and breeding behavior.

additional.name: FishBase — Corydoras habrosus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), adult size (male ~2.5 cm, female ~3.5 cm TL), native range (Venezuela, Colombia), and temperature range. Notes both Corydoras habrosus and the proposed Hoplisoma habrosus name per Dias & Britto (2024).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Corydoras Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General Corydoras care guide from Aquarium Co-Op. Supplemented husbandry details on sand substrate requirements, group size recommendations (6+), copper sensitivity, and feeding approach for small corys. No dedicated C. habrosus page available.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Corydoras habrosus species profile
additional.notes: Species profile discussing the salt-and-pepper common name, distinguishing characteristics from C. pygmaeus and C. hastatus (the pygmy cory complex), natural habitat details, and aquarium setup recommendations including fine sand and leaf litter.

additional.name: Dias & Britto (2024) — Hoplisoma revision (via Catalog of Fishes)
additional.notes: Reference for the 2024 Dias & Britto taxonomic revision proposing transfer of a large Corydoras clade including C. habrosus to the resurrected genus Hoplisoma. Adoption of the new name is pending and not yet universal across trade and hobbyist literature as of 2026.

fish.breedingNotes: Condition the group with live and frozen foods for several weeks before attempting a spawn, then trigger spawning with a water change using slightly cooler water (a drop of around 2–4°C). Eggs of about 1–2 mm are deposited on smooth surfaces and hatch in roughly 3–5 days. Fry initially require infusoria (microscopic single-celled organisms) or similarly fine foods, progressing to microworms and baby brine shrimp nauplii as they grow. Overall difficulty is moderate, driven by the very small egg and fry size and the sensitivity of early-stage juveniles to water quality.
